findings_details.title: shasum failure causes fail-closed suppression instead of fail-open pass-through
findings_details.body: On Linux systems without shasum (or any environment where shasum exits non-zero), set -euo pipefail causes the KEY assignment to abort the script with a non-zero exit code. human-emit.sh calls intent-check.sh via `if ! intent-check.sh` — a non-zero exit is treated as MATCH (line 35), suppressing the marker. This is the opposite of the documented fail-open contract (header: "claude missing or error → exit 0 (fail-open)"). Fix: wrap the KEY computation in a subshell or guard with a command existence check, and add `|| exit 0` so any hash failure exits 0 (fail-open). Also: sha256sum is the Linux equivalent of shasum -a 256; consider supporting both or checking availability.

findings_details.title: Exit code semantics are inverted from POSIX convention
findings_details.body: exit 1 means MATCH (suppress) and exit 0 means no-match or fail-open. POSIX convention is exit 0 = success. Here the "success" case (suppression found) exits 1, and the "no action" case exits 0. This inverted contract is documented in the header, but it makes the caller logic `if ! intent-check.sh` harder to read and reason about. Any new caller must know to negate the exit code. Consider reversing: exit 0 = suppress (match found), exit 1 = pass-through (no match), with the caller checking `if intent-check.sh ...; then exit 0; fi`. This aligns with standard tool conventions where exit 0 = found/true (e.g., grep).

findings_details.title: wc -l pipe with tr -dc is a fragile line-count pattern
findings_details.body: Line 75: LINES=$(wc -l <"...". | tr -dc "0-9") strips non-digits to remove wc padding spaces. A simpler and portable alternative is $(wc -l < "...") | awk "{print $1}" or just compare directly. The tr -dc pattern also strips newlines from multi-digit counts, which works but is fragile. More importantly, this same pattern appears in _call_count (line 151) in a similar form. The pattern is duplicated. Extract a shared _count_lines helper or use a consistent idiom in both places.

findings_details.title: _init_work uses cd which leaks working directory into the test runner
findings_details.body: The _init_work helper (line 33) calls `cd "$1"` which mutates the shell cwd for all subsequent code in the test script. Multiple test cases call _init_work with different directories, and some assertions depend on cwd (e.g. the non-git fallback test at line 79 calls `cd "$WORK"` directly). If any later test forgets to reset cwd or a test is added out of order, it will silently operate in the wrong directory. The git-based tests pass WORK to git operations via the directory being set, but human-emit.sh resolves repo root via `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` from the cwd at time of invocation. Fix: document that callers must cd before invoking $SCRIPT, or use a subshell per test case `(cd "$WORK"; "$SCRIPT" ...)` to isolate cwd changes.

findings_details.title: shasum not found exits non-zero, causing fail-closed suppression of marker
findings_details.body: Line 106: `printf '%s\n%s' "$COMBINED" "$MTIME" | shasum -a 256 | awk '{print $1}'` runs under set -euo pipefail. If shasum is absent (e.g. on some Linux systems where sha256sum is the command instead), the pipeline exits non-zero (127). In human-emit.sh line 35, intent-check is called as `if ! "$SCRIPT_DIR_HE/intent-check.sh" "$PAYLOAD"; then exit 0; fi`. A non-zero non-1 exit (127) satisfies `if !`, causing human-emit to suppress the marker (exit 0) — the opposite of the documented fail-open behavior. Fix: add `command -v shasum >/dev/null 2>&1 || command -v sha256sum >/dev/null 2>&1 || exit 0` before the cache key line, or use a portable fallback: `KEY=$(printf '%s\n%s' "$COMBINED" "$MTIME" | (shasum -a 256 2>/dev/null || sha256sum 2>/dev/null) | awk '{print $1}'); [ -z "$KEY" ] && exit 0`.

findings_details.title: Cache key omits payload fields beyond text+context — distinct payloads may collide
findings_details.body: The cache key at line 106 is sha256(COMBINED + mtime) where COMBINED = text + '\n' + context. Two payloads that differ only in type/url/other fields but share identical text and context will resolve to the same cache entry and get the same verdict. In practice, the intent check only evaluates the human-readable content (text and context), so the cache collision is semantically correct — the verdict for 'do the thing' is the same regardless of the URL. However, this is worth documenting clearly. Low risk but worth noting.

findings_details.title: wc -l | tr pipeline in test — will be blocked by pre-tool hook in CI
findings_details.body: Line 75: `LINES=$(wc -l <"$WORK/human-follow-up-work.md" | tr -dc '0-9')` and line 151-154: `_call_count()` uses `wc -l <"$STUB_COUNTER" | tr -dc '0-9'`. The repo's pre-tool hook blocks pipelines with `| head/tail/grep` but NOT `| tr` — so this specific pipeline passes. However, these are fragile patterns: `wc -l` on macOS includes leading whitespace which tr strips. If the hook policy tightens, these will fail. More importantly `wc -l <file | tr -dc '0-9'` is correct on both BSD and GNU. No immediate bug, but the pattern is inconsistent with the repo's redirect-to-tmp convention.

findings_details.title: Comment says 'Any other exit is treated as no match / fail open' but intent-check exit codes beyond 0/1 cause fail-closed suppression
findings_details.body: Lines 34-38: The comment documents fail-open for 'Any other exit' from intent-check.sh. However the implementation `if ! "$SCRIPT_DIR_HE/intent-check.sh" "$PAYLOAD"; then exit 0; fi` treats any non-zero exit (including error exits like 127) as 'match → suppress'. Only exit 0 from intent-check causes fall-through to emit. Any error exit from intent-check (e.g. shasum missing, set -e triggered) causes suppression, not emission. The comment is wrong; the code is fail-closed on intent-check errors. Fix: check $? explicitly, only suppress on exit 1. Example: `"$SCRIPT_DIR_HE/intent-check.sh" "$PAYLOAD"; ic_exit=$?; [ "$ic_exit" -eq 1 ] && exit 0`.
